title: ISIC-2024 SLICE-3D — the honest frontier
text:
The problem

ISIC-2024 SLICE-3D: detect malignant skin lesions in 3D total-body-photography crops.
401,059 crops · 1,042 patients · 393 malignant / 400,666 benign → a 1,021:1 imbalance.
Metric: official pAUC above 80% TPR, range [0, 0.20] (random ≈ 0.02, perfect = 0.20).
The whole problem is the imbalance: 393 positives is not much for a deep net alone.

title: ISIC-2024 SLICE-3D — the honest frontier
text:
What we deliberately ban

No external data — ISIC-2024 SLICE-3D only (no ISIC-2019/2020, no PAD-UFES, no external dermoscopy).
No generative / synthetic positives — fabricating pathology breaks label validity.
The winners’ ~0.173 used both; matching it is out of reach by construction.








Important


That impossibility is the experiment. So we change the question.

title: ISIC-2024 SLICE-3D — the honest frontier
text:
The question we actually answer

What is the best pAUC achievable per unit of inference cost — on SLICE-3D data alone?


Not one private-LB number → a quality-vs-cost Pareto frontier.
Axes: pAUC vs params · FLOPs · CPU latency.
A model only counts if it earns its cost.

title: ISIC-2024 SLICE-3D — the honest frontier
text:
CV discipline — the whole game

Leaky CV sent teams ~200 places down on the private split.
Patient-grouped, target-stratified folds (5, SEED 42), frozen once into data/folds.parquet.
No patient straddles folds. One metric, one implementation (src/cv.py), == official scorer.
cv-guardian agent holds veto power over splits & metric.

title: ISIC-2024 SLICE-3D — the honest frontier
text:
Where the signal lives

The dominant signal is the ugly-duckling sign: how anomalous a lesion is vs the same patient’s moles.
Patient-relative features (pdev_ / prank_ / pxc_) = ~65% of GBDT gain.
Top features: tbp_lv_H (hue, univariate AUC 0.81), pdev_tbp_lv_H, pdev_clin_size.

title: ISIC-2024 SLICE-3D — the honest frontier
text:
Every complex idea lost


Learned MoE gate 0.150 — below tabular alone.
Meta-LGBM stacker 0.171 < trivial 0.174. Image embeddings: hurt. Heavy transformers: collapse.

title: ISIC-2024 SLICE-3D — the honest frontier
text:
Why simpler won

Every learned add-on must fit parameters from the same 393 positives the experts already used.
A rank-average has zero parameters → cannot overfit the folds.
At extreme imbalance: bias toward zero-parameter fusion + small backbones — also the cheapest.

title: ISIC-2024 SLICE-3D — the honest frontier
text:
Credit where due

1st — Ilya Novoselskiy (EVA-02 + EdgeNeXt + GBDT, private 0.17264; external + ~30k synthetic — both banned here; synthetic added only +0.0007 in their own ablation).
2nd — uchiyama33, 3rd — kyohei-123.
Public lineage: greysky (tabular), snnclsr, motono0223, andreasbis (CNN-preds-as-feature), richolson.

title: ISIC-2024 SLICE-3D — the honest frontier
text:
Conclusion

Defensible claim: SOTA among single-dataset, no-external-data, no-synthetic solutions, on a quality–efficiency frontier.
Best constrained point: OOF pAUC 0.17376 @ 15.8 M / 2.46 GFLOPs / 61 ms CPU.
Validation discipline + an explicit cost axis give the result teeth.
Reproducible: SEED=42, config-driven, make folds → gbdt → vision → frontier → site.

title: Reproducibility
text:
Hardware
A single NVIDIA RTX 4070 Ti SUPER (16 GB). The whole study fits comfortably in the 2-day budget because of per-epoch negative undersampling:

an “epoch” sees all 393 positives + a fresh ~equal sample of negatives → a few thousand images,
≈ 4.5 s / epoch,
a full 5-fold backbone trains in ≈ 8 minutes.

CPU latency on the frontier is measured single-threaded (src/efficiency.py) so the cost axis reflects a worst-case deployable setting.

title: Reproducibility
text:
The exact recipe
The whole pipeline is driven by make targets (each wraps a python -m src.* invocation). make folds must run first — nothing trains until the no-leak guarantee passes.
# 0. fetch SLICE-3D into data/ (accept the comp rules on Kaggle once, else 403)
make data

# 1. freeze folds + metric sanity check  — ALWAYS FIRST
make folds                                   # python -m src.cv

# 2. tabular expert -> OOF + pAUC (banks ~90% of achievable score)
make gbdt                                     # python -m src.gbdt

# 3. image expert(s) as a stacked feature (one config = one frontier point)
make vision CFG=configs/vision/convnextv2_nano_r224.yaml
#   ... repeat across the 12-backbone sweep ...

# 4. fuse + log the frontier (params / FLOPs / CPU latency next to pAUC)
make frontier                                 # python -m src.stack ; reports/frontier.py

# 5. spine tests: metric anchors, no-leak, official-equivalence  (9/9)
make test

# 6. build this site (renders to ../docs for GitHub Pages)
make site
Each run is logged under experiments/ (one config + one CSV row per run, seeds fixed) and the frontier is appended to reports/frontier.csv / reports/frontier_cost.csv.

TipWhy these numbers are trustworthy

title: Reproducibility
text:
Determinism caveats (honest)
Full bit-exact determinism across GPUs/driver versions is not guaranteed for deep-learning training (cuDNN nondeterminism, atomic reductions). We fix SEED = 42, log every config, and report OOF pAUC to 5 decimals; the GBDT and stack numbers are fully deterministic from the frozen folds, and the image numbers reproduce to within fold-variance (~±0.008 std, far smaller than the effects we report). This is stated plainly rather than overclaimed.

Continue to Credits →

title: Submission & Leaderboard
text: ISIC-2024 was a Kaggle CODE competition: you do not upload a CSV of predictions — you submit a notebook that Kaggle re-runs on a hidden test set. The public test that shipped with the data is a 3-row placeholder, so a true private score cannot be computed locally. This page lays that out honestly: what we can produce today, what the CV implies, and the exact path to a real late-submission score.

title: Submission & Leaderboard
text:
How scoring actually works






ImportantA CODE competition scores a notebook, not a file




You attach a notebook to the competition. On submit, Kaggle executes it against the hidden test set (the real evaluation images), and scores the submission.csv it writes.
The test-image.hdf5 and test-metadata.csv shipped in the data bundle are a 3-row placeholder — just enough to let your notebook run end-to-end without crashing.
Therefore no honest private pAUC can be produced offline. Anyone reporting a “private score” computed on their own machine is reporting a number off three rows. We do not.



The placeholder we hold on disk, verified directly:








Artifact
What ships locally
Reality on Kaggle




data/test-image.hdf5
≈ 11 KB, 3 images (ISIC_0015657, ISIC_0015729, ISIC_0015740)
tens of thousands of hidden crops


data/test-metadata.csv
3 rows (header + 3)
the full hidden cohort’s metadata


data/sample_submission.csv
3 rows, constant target = 0.3
format template only



So the only thing a local run can prove is that the inference pipeline is wired correctly and emits a format-valid file — which is exactly what we did.

title: Submission & Leaderboard
text:
What we produced today
We generated a format-valid submission.csv from the saved bagged GBDT — 50 boosters (5 folds × 5 LightGBM seeds + 5 CatBoost seeds, persisted in experiments/gbdt_boosters.joblib) — via a single command:
python -m src.submit          # GBDT-only; reads experiments/gbdt_boosters.joblib
Inference mirrors the leak-free training transform exactly: every booster carries its own per-fold feature state, predict_gbdt re-applies that state before predicting, and the per-fold scores are averaged. The output is the required two-column schema:
isic_id,target
ISIC_0015657,0.41827...
ISIC_0015729,0.06913...
ISIC_0015740,0.22540...








Column
Type
Meaning




isic_id
string
lesion-crop identifier, one row per test image


target
float
predicted malignancy score (rank-comparable; pAUC is rank-based, so calibration is irrelevant)









What this validates — and what it does not. Running python -m src.submit on the 3-row placeholder confirms the inference path end-to-end: boosters load, the fold-local feature transform replays without leakage, and the writer emits a valid 2-column file. It does not produce a real score — that requires Kaggle to run this on the hidden test (a late submission, since the competition is closed).

title: Submission & Leaderboard
section: Honest caveat: stack vs. tabular submission
text:
Honest caveat: stack vs. tabular submission
The tabular GBDT submission is ready today. The full stack submission is not — and we say why plainly.






WarningWhat the stack needs that we do not yet persist



A stack submission must run image inference on the hidden test set, which requires the trained per-fold image checkpoints. We persisted the image expert’s OOF probabilities and embeddings (enough to score and ablate the stack on CV), but not the per-fold model weights. So:

Ready now: GBDT-only submission.csv (python -m src.submit) — validates the full inference path on the placeholder.
Documented next step: a stack notebook that loads saved image checkpoints, scores the hidden test, and rank-blends with the GBDT. src/submit.py already exposes the hook (--image-scores ... --image-weight ...); it is intentionally unwired rather than fabricating image predictions until the checkpoints exist.




The late-submission path






flowchart LR
    A["Trained models<br/>GBDT boosters (done)<br/>+ per-fold image ckpts (next)"] --> B
    B["Upload as a<br/>Kaggle Dataset"] --> C
    C["Inference notebook<br/>attach competition + dataset<br/>load models, predict hidden test<br/>rank-blend GBDT + image"] --> D
    D["Write submission.csv<br/>(isic_id, target)"] --> E["Submit (late)<br/>→ real private pAUC"]

    A -. "image branch<br/>needs saved ckpts" .-> C
    style A fill:#0b6e4f,color:#fff
    style E fill:#1e8449,color:#fff




Figure 1: The documented path to a real private score via a Kaggle late submission. Steps 1–3 are done today (GBDT-only); the dashed image branch is the persisted next step.





Concretely, to obtain a genuine private score:

Train + persist per-fold image checkpoints (src/vision/train.py) — the only missing artifact; OOF probs/embeddings already exist.
Upload the trained models (GBDT joblib + image checkpoints) as a private Kaggle dataset.
Author an inference notebook that attaches the competition data + the model dataset, runs predict_test(...) with --image-scores, and writes the 2-column submission.csv.
Submit it as a late submission — Kaggle runs it on the hidden test and returns the real private pAUC, which we expect to land in the 0.155–0.165 band projected above.







The tabular-only path (steps 2–4 without the image branch) is runnable immediately and is the honest, ready-today deliverable; the stack path is a clean, fully-specified follow-up rather than a hidden gap.




Continue to Results →

title: Methods
text: This page is deliberately verbose and bottom-up. We start with the single most important and most misunderstood thing — the metric — and build up to the full hybrid pipeline.

title: Methods
section: The metric: partial AUC above 80% TPR
text:
The metric: partial AUC above 80% TPR

Why not accuracy, and why not plain AUC?
At 0.098% prevalence, accuracy is meaningless (predict “benign” always → 99.9% accurate, zero malignant caught). Plain ROC-AUC is better — it is threshold-free and prevalence-robust — but it averages performance over all operating points, including useless low-sensitivity ones. A clinical triage tool only matters where it catches almost every cancer: the high-sensitivity tail.


The definition
The official ISIC-2024 metric is the partial area under the ROC curve, restricted to the region where the true-positive rate (sensitivity) is at least 80%:
\[
\text{pAUC}_{80} \;=\; \int_{\text{TPR}=0.80}^{1.0} \text{TPR}\; d(\text{FPR})
\]
Equivalently: integrate the ROC curve only over the band TPR ∈ [0.80, 1.0] (FPR ≤ 0.20), then report the area in TPR-units × FPR-units. Because the band is 0.20 tall, the score is bounded in [0, 0.20]:

random classifier ≈ 0.02,
perfect classifier = 0.20.

A move from 0.169 → 0.174 is therefore a +2.9% relative gain on a small absolute scale — small absolute moves are large relative moves, and we phrase improvements that way throughout.


Two pitfalls we pinned down up front






WarningThe 80% vs 88% trap



The public ISIC metrics repository’s README mentions a 88% TPR threshold (which would bound the score in [0, 0.12]), but the live competition used 80%. Mixing the two makes any number incomparable. We pin 80% for every leaderboard-comparable claim, and our scorer is hard-coded to it.


We do not re-implement the metric loosely. src/cv.py:pauc_above_tpr is the only function allowed to compute it, and it is proven numerically identical to the vendored official scorer (src/metric_official.py, © 2024 N. R. Kurtansky, MSKCC) by a unit test (tests/test_cv.py, part of the 9/9 passing suite).

title: Methods
section: The CV spine: the whole game
text:
The CV spine: the whole game
In ISIC-2024, leaky cross-validation sent teams ~200 places down on the private split. Validation is not a step here — it is the spine everything hangs on.

Patient-grouped, target-stratified folds. We use StratifiedGroupKFold (5 folds, SEED =

with the patient as the group and the malignant label as the stratification target.


Grouped → no patient straddles folds (a patient’s lesions are correlated; a single straddling patient leaks).
Stratified → each fold holds 77–83 of the 393 positives, so no fold is starved of signal.

Frozen once. The split is written to data/folds.parquet and read, unchanged, by every expert and by the stack. There is exactly one split in the entire project.
Hard no-leak guarantee + veto. The cv-guardian agent has veto power over anything touching the split or the metric: if a change would leak patients across folds, recompute the metric independently, or compare models on mismatched splits, it must block. This is the anti-shakeup discipline that makes our OOF number trustworthy.







Out-of-fold (OOF) predictions. For each fold we train on the other four and predict on the held-out fold; concatenating the five held-out predictions gives one prediction per row, none of which ever saw its own row in training. We score pAUC once on this full OOF vector. Because pAUC is non-additive across folds, the full-OOF number (0.17376) differs trivially from the mean of per-fold pAUCs (0.17487) — both are reported.

title: Methods
text:
Tabular feature engineering
The predictive signal lives largely in the tabular metadata. src/features.py builds, from ISIC-2024 columns only (no external data, every feature leak-checked):

Geometry & size ratios — normalized lesion size, perimeter, area, eccentricity, axis ratios, border composites; raw size is strong, ratios make it scale-robust.
Color / hue / luminance contrasts — tbp_lv_H (hue angle), lesion-vs-skin contrast, color uniformity, ΔA/ΔB color deltas. Hue alone reaches univariate AUC 0.81.
Border & shape composites — irregularity and asymmetry combinations.
3D position — the lesion’s location on the body surface (TBP gives true 3D coordinates).
Patient-relative “ugly-duckling” deviations — the decisive block. For each lesion and each base feature, how anomalous is it relative to the same patient’s other lesions?

pdev_* — deviation (signed/standardized distance from the patient’s own mean),
prank_* — within-patient percentile rank,
pxc_* — patient × category interactions (e.g. hue × body location).
These encode the clinical “ugly-duckling sign” directly. They turn out to be ~65% of total GBDT gain (see Results and Ablations).

Per-fold smoothed target encoding — for high-cardinality categoricals (e.g. location), computed inside each fold’s training data only and applied to the held-out fold, with smoothing toward the global mean.







ImportantLeak-safety of features



Every patient-relative statistic and every target encoding is computed fold-locally (from training rows only) and applied to held-out rows — so no held-out information ever flows into a feature. The cv-guardian audits this. Patient-relative deviations are safe because they use the patient’s own benign moles, which are in the same fold by construction (patients don’t straddle folds).

title: Methods
text:
The GBDT expert
src/gbdt.py trains the tabular expert. Two stories matter here — one cautionary, one constructive.

The underfit bug (and the fix)
Our first GBDT scored 0.09941 — barely 5× random. The cause was a classic imbalance foot-gun: is_unbalance=True up-weighted the 393 positives ~1000×, and the LightGBM early-stopping was driven by built-in AUC, which under that weighting peaked at best_iter = 1 — the model essentially never trained.
The fix, in two parts:

Drop is_unbalance — let undersampling (below) handle the imbalance instead of extreme instance weights.
Early-stop on the official pAUC — a custom feval in src/gbdt.py evaluates pAUC@80%TPR directly, so early stopping optimizes the metric we are actually scored on.

This single change took the model from 0.09941 → 0.11826 (+0.019). See Ablations → Tabular.


The production recipe
The final tabular expert (greysky-lineage hyperparameters; see Credits) is a bagged ensemble:

Manual undersampling to ~1% (neg_ratio = 0.01 → ~100 negatives per positive, ~4k rows per fold/seed) so each booster trains fast and is not drowned by benigns.
5-seed bagging (seeds 12/22/32/42/52) of LightGBM, averaged on rank.
CatBoost rank-blend — a second GBDT family bagged the same way; the final tabular OOF is 0.8·rank(LightGBM-bag) + 0.2·rank(CatBoost-bag). CatBoost is the weaker family here, so an equal 0.5 blend dilutes (0.16809); 0.2 is the swept optimum (0.16890).

Result: OOF pAUC 0.16890 at essentially zero inference cost (0.86 M “params” of trees, ~0 GFLOPs, 0.02 ms/img). This is the efficient anchor of the whole frontier.

title: Methods
text:
The image experts
src/vision/* trains the deep protagonist. Each backbone is one point on the efficiency frontier, measured by the efficiency-auditor.

The recipe (shared across backbones)

Small, natural-image-pretrained backbones at 128 px (and 224 px for the resolution axis). Pretraining is ImageNet / FCMAE only — no skin-cancer labels, so the no-external-data claim survives (pretrained weights are allowed; external training data is not).
Per-epoch negative undersampling to ~1:1 (neg_ratio, pos_mult). Each epoch sees all positives + a fresh random ~equal sample of negatives, so an “epoch” is only a few thousand images and runs in ~4.5 s. A full 5-fold backbone trains in ~8 minutes. Re-sampling the negatives each epoch means the model still sees the whole benign distribution over time.
Loss: BCE with label smoothing 0.05 (softens the 393 hard positives).
Augmentation: the transV2 policy (flips, rotations, color jitter within reason — classical only, no generative augmentation).
EMA(0.995) + mixup(α=0.2). Weight EMA stabilizes the noisy undersampled updates; mixup regularizes. EMA decay matters: at ~23 steps/epoch, decay 0.999 (time-constant ~1000 steps) lags the weights and hurts (fold-0: 0.146 vs 0.156); 0.995 + warmup helps (0.163). Pinned at 0.995.
Resolution as a frontier axis. We keep both convnextv2_nano@128 (0.15311) and @224 (0.15821) as distinct points, because resolution trades accuracy for cost.



The 12-backbone frontier sweep
We swept a family spanning the cost axis from ~2.5 M to ~294 M params: mnv4_small, effvit_b0, starnet_s1, fastvit_t8, effnetv2_b0, ghostnetv3, vit_tiny, vit_small, convnextv2_nano (128 px and 224 px), convnextv2_tiny, swinv2_tiny, eva02_small, and the heavy anchor mnv5_300m. The clear lesson (see Results and Ablations): heavy transformers collapse to near-random at 393 positives (SwinV2 @256 → 0.104, EVA-02 @336 → 0.100), while the small convnextv2_nano is the sweet spot.


Self-supervised pretraining — the principled “one-up” (future work)
The cleanest in-constraint way to exploit the 400k+ unlabeled crops — without importing external data or fabricating positives — is self-supervised pretraining (MAE / DINOv2-style) on the crops themselves, then fine-tuning on the 393 labels. This substitutes our own unlabeled data for the external data we banned, and stays fully inside the rules. We present it as the principled next step rather than a completed result; it is the most promising lever we did not exhaust in the 2-day budget.

title: Methods
text:
The stack (combiner)
src/stack.py fuses the experts. We compared three combiners on the same OOF vectors:








Combiner
What it does
OOF pAUC




Rank-average
average the percentile ranks of GBDT and image OOF — param-free
0.17376


Meta-LightGBM
a second-level GBDT over the expert OOFs
0.17108


Learned per-lesion gate (MoE)
a network picks expert weights per lesion
0.15007



We also tried adding the image embedding (PCA components) and an image-space ugly-duckling feature into the GBDT; both hurt (< 0.1738).






NoteWhy the trivial combiner wins at 393 positives



A rank-average has zero parameters to fit, so it cannot overfit the validation folds. Every learned combiner has to estimate its parameters from the same 393 positives the experts already used — there is simply not enough positive signal left to learn a better fusion than “average the ranks.” The learned gate even falls below the tabular expert alone (0.150 < 0.169). This is the project’s recurring theme, and it is a result, not a disappointment: at this scale, simpler is both cheaper and more accurate. Full numbers in Ablations.

title: Methods
text:
Efficiency as a first-class axis
Every reported model logs params, FLOPs, and single-thread CPU latency (src/efficiency.py) alongside its pAUC, so each model is one point on a quality-vs-cost plane. The Pareto-optimal set — points that no other model beats on both axes — is the headline figure. A model that does not earn its cost does not stay in the pipeline. See Results.

Continue to Results →

title: ISIC-2024 SLICE-3D
text:
Pakistan.AI · Neural Networks · CSIE, NYUST


Detecting the 393 malignant lesions hidden among 400,666 benign crops (a 1,021:1 imbalance) — using only the ISIC-2024 SLICE-3D dataset, no external dermoscopy, and no synthetic/generative positives.

The claim under test: state-of-the-art among single-dataset, no-external-data, no-synthetic solutions — reported not as one number, but as a quality-vs-cost Pareto frontier.

title: ISIC-2024 SLICE-3D
text:
Abstract
We study the ISIC-2024 SLICE-3D skin-lesion task — classifying ~401,059 lesion crops extracted from 3D total-body photography (TBP) as malignant or benign — under a deliberately strict regime. The competition’s top private-leaderboard solutions reached pAUC ≈ 0.173, but they did so by importing external dermoscopy archives and roughly 30,000 diffusion-synthesized malignant lesions. We ban both. On medical images, training or validating on fabricated pathology is a label-validity hole; and importing external data breaks the “single dataset” premise. Removing those two crutches is exactly what our experiment isolates.
Because the absolute leaderboard number is then out of reach by construction, we re-pose the problem as a Pareto frontier: the best official pAUC above 80% TPR achievable per unit of inference cost (parameters, FLOPs, and single-thread CPU latency), on SLICE-3D data alone. The architecture is a hybrid: a LightGBM tabular expert over intrinsic engineered features (geometry, color/hue contrast, size ratios, and patient-relative “ugly-duckling” deviations) provides the efficient anchor; a small ImageNet-pretrained image backbone contributes an out-of-fold (OOF) malignancy probability that is fused into the final score by a trivial rank-average combiner.
The honest punchline: every increase in model complexity we tried lost. The trivial 2-way rank-average beats a meta-LightGBM stacker, a learned per-lesion gate, and the addition of image embeddings; the small convnextv2_nano backbone beats heavy transformers (SwinV2, EVA-02) that collapse to near-random at 393 positives. Our best frontier point — rank-avg[GBDT, convnextv2_nano@224] — reaches OOF pAUC 0.17376 at 15.8 M params / 2.46 GFLOPs / 61 ms CPU per image.

title: ISIC-2024 SLICE-3D
text:
Scope honesty






ImportantWhat we are — and are not — claiming




We are not claiming to beat the unconstrained leaderboard winner’s private pAUC ≈ 0.1755. That score used external data + ~30k synthetic positives; matching it under our rules is impossible by construction, and that is precisely the point of the experiment.
We are claiming SOTA within the no-external-data / no-synthetic / single-dataset class, on a transparent quality-vs-cost frontier, with leak-audited CV.
The 1st-place team’s own ablation shows their ~30k synthetic lesions added only +0.0007 pAUC — a sobering data point for the “just add synthetic data” reflex on a 393-positive task.
Our OOF number is, if anything, a more honest predictor of generalization than a leaderboard-tuned number, because no part of our pipeline ever saw out-of-distribution data. Expect a typical public→private drop of ~0.013–0.021 on this task; we report the OOF CV and state that projection openly.









NoteLicensing note on figures



Every figure on this site is our own (generated by reports/eda/make_eda.py, reports/perf/make_perf.py, reports/frontier.py) plus Mermaid diagrams. We deliberately use no external copyrighted images — appropriate for a medical-imaging deliverable and for the dataset’s CC BY-NC 4.0 terms.



Continue to Data & EDA →

title: Data & Exploratory Analysis
text:
ISIC-2024 SLICE-3D is a corpus of lesion crops extracted from 3D Total-Body Photography (TBP) — full-body imaging that captures every visible mole on a patient at once. Each row is one lesion, with a small square JPEG crop plus a rich tabular record of measurements computed by the TBP vendor’s software (geometry, color, 3D position) and clinical context (age, sex, body site).







Fact
Value




Lesion crops (rows)
401,059


Malignant lesions
393


Benign lesions
400,666


Prevalence
0.098% — about 1 malignant per 1,021 crops


Unique patients
1,042


Patients with ≥1 malignant lesion
259 (24.9%)


Columns
55 (35 float, 18 string, 2 int)


Real (non-leak) feature columns
36


Imaging
every crop is a TBP tile (TBP tile: close-up); 2 subtypes (3D: XP, 3D: white)


Source institutions
7 (MSKCC, Hospital Clínic Barcelona, Univ. Basel, Frazer Institute/UQ, ACEMID MIA, MedUni Vienna, Univ. Athens)









ImportantThe imbalance is the whole problem



At 0.098% prevalence, a model that predicts “benign” for everything is 99.9% accurate and clinically useless. This is why the task is scored by partial AUC above 80% TPR, not plain accuracy or even plain AUC — see Methods → The metric. It is also why patient-grouped, target-stratified cross-validation is non-negotiable: with only 393 positives, a single leaked patient or an unlucky split can swing the score more than any model choice.




The crops are variable-size square JPEGs, ranging from 61 px to 239 px on a side (median / mean ≈ 131 / 133 px), and only ~2.8 KB each. Two consequences for our image experts:

128 px ≈ the native median. Training at 128 px is essentially training at the crops’ natural resolution — no information is being thrown away, and epochs are cheap.
224 px upsamples. The 224 px model does not see extra pixels; it sees the same lesion interpolated larger. So the measured 224 px gain (0.15311 → 0.15821) is a capacity / training-dynamics effect (a bigger receptive field for the pretrained backbone, more effective augmentation), not extra image detail. We keep both points because resolution is a frontier axis, and we report this caveat honestly.




Only three real feature columns have any missing values; everything else is 100% complete.



Column
Missing

2.87%

1.44%

0.70%









WarningTrain-only LEAK columns — we drop them



Several columns exist only in the training split and encode the answer or post-biopsy pathology. Feeding any of them to a model is leakage; we use them for EDA framing only and drop them before training:
iddx_full, iddx_1 … iddx_5 (iddx_1 is literally Benign / Malignant / Indeterminate), mel_mitotic_index, mel_thick_mm, lesion_id, and tbp_lv_dnn_lesion_confidence (a vendor model’s own confidence — not available at inference and effectively a label proxy).

title: Data & Exploratory Analysis
text:
The nine EDA figures
All figures are generated by reports/eda/make_eda.py (read-only on data/, SEED = 42, DPI 150, Okabe-Ito colorblind-safe palette).

Fig 1 — Class imbalance & fold stratification






Figure 1: Class imbalance and per-fold positive counts.



400,666 benign vs 393 malignant (0.098% prevalence). The five patient-grouped CV folds each hold 77–83 positives (~0.096–0.103% prevalence per fold), so stratification is tight and no fold is starved of signal — a precondition for trustworthy OOF estimates.


Fig 2 — Patient structure






Figure 2: Per-patient lesion counts; malignant-carrying patients hold most of the data.



1,042 patients with a heavily right-skewed lesion count (median 241, max 9,184). The 259 patients who carry at least one malignant lesion hold 89% of all crops — most data lives with the high-risk patients. This is the structural reason splits must be patient-grouped: a patient’s lesions are correlated, so any patient straddling folds leaks information.


Fig 3 — Demographics






Figure 3: Age, sex, and body-site malignant rates.



Malignant lesions skew older (peak ~60–75), males contribute most crops, and head/neck stands out:

Head/neck carries ~7× the baseline malignant rate of any other site, despite being the smallest site — a strong site prior the model should respect.


Fig 4 — Lesion size






Figure 4: Clinical diameter and TBP area: malignant ~2× larger.



On both clinical longest diameter and TBP area, the malignant distribution is shifted ~2× larger than benign. Raw lesion size is a strong, cheap univariate signal — and, as the ablations show, its patient-relative version is even stronger.


Fig 5 — Color & border “ugly-duckling” signals






Figure 5: Hue, contrast, border/color irregularity, eccentricity.



Hue angle (tbp_lv_H) separates the classes most cleanly — univariate AUC 0.81 on a single feature. Lesion-skin contrast, border/color irregularity, and eccentricity all shift toward higher values for malignant, confirming the TBP color/geometry features carry real, independent signal.


Fig 6 — Correlation of key TBP features






Figure 6: Correlation heatmap of 21 core geometry/color features.



The core features form tight blocks (a size group: diameter / area / perimeter / minor-axis; a color group: A / B / ΔA / ΔB). The GBDT therefore sees substantial redundancy — useful for robustness, but a sign that a handful of axes capture most of the variance.


Fig 7 — Sample lesion crops






Figure 7: Eight malignant (top) vs eight benign (bottom) crops.



Malignant lesions tend to be larger, darker, and more color-varied — but the visual overlap with benign is large. That overlap is exactly why a learned image expert is needed on top of the tabular features, and equally why the image expert alone (0.15821) cannot beat the tabular expert (0.16890).


Fig 8 — Image-embedding class separation






Figure 8: PCA-50 → t-SNE of ConvNeXtV2-nano OOF embeddings.



A t-SNE of the small CNN’s OOF embeddings (all 393 positives + 3,000 random negatives): the malignant points concentrate into a recognizable region of feature space rather than scattering uniformly. Even a small CNN learns a malignancy-relevant representation — evidence for stacking its OOF probability into the GBDT.


Fig 9 — Ugly-duckling illustration






Figure 9: Three patients: the malignant lesion sits at the top of its own size distribution.



For three patients with one malignant lesion among many benign ones, the malignant lesion sits at the top of its own patient’s size distribution (98th–100th percentile). This is the concrete picture behind the within-patient deviation features.






TipThe ugly-duckling sign, quantified




392 of 393 malignant lesions come from a patient who also has benign lesions — so almost every positive can be judged against that patient’s own “normal” moles.
On clin_size_long_diam_mm, the malignant lesion sits at a median 88th within-patient percentile; 45.7% of malignant lesions are in the top 10% of their own patient’s lesion sizes. This is the quantitative basis for the engineered patient-relative features — not folklore, but a measured effect.

title: Data & Exploratory Analysis
text:
The four most striking takeaways

The imbalance is the whole problem. 0.098% prevalence is why the task uses partial-AUC-above-80%-TPR and why patient-grouped, target-stratified CV is mandatory. The folds confirm 77–83 positives each with zero patients straddling folds.
Tabular signal is real and cheap. Single features already separate well: hue tbp_lv_H reaches univariate AUC 0.81, and lesion size shows a clean ~2× malignant shift. This is the evidence base for the LightGBM-first architecture.
The ugly-duckling sign is quantitatively present. A lesion’s deviation from its own patient’s distribution is well-motivated — and it turns out to be the single dominant signal in the trained model (~65% of GBDT gain; see Results).
The small CNN adds an orthogonal axis. Its embeddings cluster the positives despite heavy visual overlap in raw crops — justifying a stacked image OOF probability, while head/neck’s ~7× elevated rate flags a site prior worth respecting.


Continue to Methods →

In that scored band the stack sits above both single experts: the visual confirmation that the +0.0049 lift is concentrated where it is scored, not smeared across useless low-sensitivity operating points.

ImportantFold variance is the dominant uncertainty — and the stack tames it



At 393 positives, per-fold pAUC swings by ~0.025 (tabular ranges 0.154 → 0.180 across folds), so fold-to-fold variance is larger than the gap between models. This is why a single-fold number would be untrustworthy. The strongest evidence the +0.0049 is real and not a fluke: the stack has the lowest std (0.0073) and wins or ties on every fold. It is both the most accurate and the most stable estimator we have.

title: Results
text:
Score separation & calibration






Figure 9: Left: stack score histogram by class. Right: reliability curve.



Malignant score-mass concentrates near 1.0 and benign mass near 0.0, with the mid-range overlap that the partial-AUC region penalizes. The reliability curve shows the model separates classes well by rank but is far from probability-calibrated (observed malignant fraction stays ≈0 even in the top bin). This is expected and fine: under 0.1% prevalence and a rank-based metric, calibration is irrelevant to pAUC. It is a feature of the design, not a defect.

title: Results
text:
The honest CV → private projection






WarningWhat to expect on a hidden test set



The field saw a roughly 0.013–0.021 public→private drop on this task even with clean CV — driven by the 393-positive scale and (for the winners) heavy reliance on external/synthetic data. Our headline is OOF CV 0.1738 (stack) / 0.1689 (tabular); a fair private-test projection is headline minus ~0.01–0.02. We state this openly. Because no part of our pipeline ever saw out-of-distribution data, our OOF number is, if anything, a more honest predictor of generalization than a leaderboard-tuned score.



Continue to Ablations →

title: Ablations & Negative Results
text:
ImportantThe thesis of this page



We log negative results honestly; we never quietly drop a model that did not help. The headline finding of the entire study is visible here in one sentence: almost every increase in model complexity lost. At 393 positives, the efficient, trivial choices — a small backbone, a rank-average combiner, intrinsic tabular features — are not just cheaper, they are also more accurate. That negative result is the contribution.

title: Ablations & Negative Results
section: Why “everything complex lost” is the finding
text:
Why “everything complex lost” is the finding
There is a structural reason every learned add-on failed: each one must estimate its parameters from the same 393 positives the base experts already consumed. A rank-average has zero parameters, so it cannot overfit the validation folds; a meta-learner, a gate, or extra embedding dimensions all have parameters to fit and not enough fresh positive signal to fit them well. The result generalizes a classical lesson to the extreme-imbalance regime: when positives are scarce, model selection should bias hard toward zero-parameter fusion and small backbones. That is the defensible, reusable claim this study contributes — and it is also the cheapest option, which is why it sits on the efficiency frontier.

Continue to Reproducibility →

title: Credits & Positioning
text: This project stands on the shoulders of the ISIC-2024 community. We are explicit about what we reused versus what is ours, and we credit the people whose work taught us the task.

title: Credits & Positioning
text:
The leaderboard champions
We did not beat the unconstrained private leaderboard, and we are not trying to — we banned the two ingredients (external data, synthetic positives) that the top teams relied on. Their results are the benchmark our constrained claim is positioned against, and they deserve full credit.






Note1st place — Ilya Novoselskiy



Private pAUC 0.17264 (post-competition reports cite ~0.1755 for the best unconstrained configuration). An ensemble of EVA-02 + EdgeNeXt image models fused with a GBDT tabular stack. Crucially, the solution used external ISIC-archive dermoscopy data and ~30,000 Stable-Diffusion-synthesized malignant lesions — both of which we deliberately ban.
The most instructive detail for our study: their own ablation reports the ~30k synthetic lesions added only +0.0007 pAUC. On a 393-positive task, fabricating 30k positives bought almost nothing — a sobering data point for the “just add synthetic data” reflex, and part of why we judged the label-validity risk not worth taking.








Note2nd & 3rd place




2nd — uchiyama33 — strong image + tabular ensemble; a source of the EMA + mixup recipe we adopted for the small backbone.
3rd — kyohei-123 — likewise a top image/tabular blend.

Both are credited for the public methodology that shaped the field’s image-expert recipe.

title: Credits & Positioning
text:
The public-notebook lineage
Our tabular feature engineering and overall recipe descend directly from the public Kaggle notebooks. We learned the task from them and reuse their ideas with attribution:







Source
What we learned / reused




greysky — “only tabular”
The LightGBM hyperparameters and the tabular-first thesis. Our lgb_bag params are greysky-lineage; the bagging + undersampling structure follows this work.


snnclsr
Tabular feature-engineering patterns and CV structure.


motono0223
Image-baseline training recipe (small backbones, undersampling).


andreasbis — “CNN preds as features”
The core stacking idea: feed an image model’s OOF probability into the GBDT as a feature.


richolson
Additional baseline ideas and ensembling practice.



We also vendored the official ISIC-2024 scorer (src/metric_official.py, © 2024 N. R. Kurtansky, MSKCC) verbatim and attributed, purely to prove our metric matches it.

title: Credits & Positioning
section: What we reused vs. what is ours
text:
What we reused vs. what is ours






TipReused (with attribution)




The tabular-first thesis and LightGBM hyperparameter lineage (greysky).
The CNN-preds-as-a-feature stacking idea (andreasbis).
The EMA + mixup small-backbone recipe (2nd/4th-place public methodology).
The official pAUC scorer (Kurtansky / MSKCC), vendored to verify our implementation.









ImportantOur own contributions




The efficiency frontier as the deliverable. We reframe the task from “one private-LB number” to a quality-vs-cost Pareto frontier across params, FLOPs, and CPU latency — so a model only counts if it earns its cost. Every reported model is one measured point.
The no-external / no-synthetic discipline, made rigorous. A locked scope (docs/DECISIONS.md), a cv-guardian with veto power, and a hard no-leak guarantee — turning “we didn’t cheat” into something audited, not asserted.
The image-space ugly-duckling. Extending the tabular ugly-duckling idea into the image expert (a within-patient image-deviation signal) — tried, measured, and reported even though it did not help, because honest negatives are the point.
A complete, honest negative-results catalogue. Meta-stacker, learned MoE gate, image embeddings, heavy transformers, over-strong EMA — every one logged with its number. The finding that complexity consistently lost at 393 positives is itself the contribution.

title: Credits & Positioning
text:
Where our claim sits






WarningHonest positioning




We claim SOTA within the no-external-data / no-synthetic / single-dataset class, on a transparent efficiency frontier with leak-audited CV.
We do not claim to beat the champions’ unconstrained ~0.1755 — that used the very ingredients we banned, and matching it is impossible by construction.
Our best constrained point is OOF pAUC 0.17376 at 15.8 M params / 2.46 GFLOPs / 61 ms CPU. The honest private-test projection is that minus ~0.01–0.02 (see Results).
